content:
FINAL ANDROID “FAILED TO DOWNLOAD REMOTE UPDATE” REPAIR — EXECUTE NOW

The Android app still crashes with:

Uncaught Error:
java.io.IOException: Failed to download remote update

This is not accepted as complete. Repair the Expo/EAS Update configuration end-to-end.

DO NOT:
- provide narrative only
- ask for credentials unless a real command returns 401/403
- publish another update blindly
- claim success without installing and opening the APK on Android
- change only JavaScript without verifying the native build configuration

==================================================
1. CAPTURE THE REAL FAILURE
==================================================

Connect the affected Android device/emulator and capture the complete native error:

adb logcat -c
adb logcat | grep -i -E "expo|updates|IOException|download|manifest|asset|runtime|ssl|certificate|unknownhost|timeout"

Return the exact failing:
- update URL
- HTTP status
- exception
- update ID
- runtimeVersion
- channel
- missing or failed asset URL

Do not hide or summarize the root exception.

==================================================
2. AUDIT EXPO CONFIGURATION
==================================================

Run:

npx expo config --type public
npx expo-doctor
eas project:info
eas channel:list
eas branch:list
eas update:list --limit 20
eas build:list --platform android --limit 10

Verify all of these values:

- expo.owner
- expo.slug
- expo.extra.eas.projectId
- expo.updates.url
- expo.runtimeVersion
- android.package
- production build channel
- channel-to-branch mapping
- latest update runtimeVersion
- latest APK runtimeVersion
- latest update platform includes Android

There must be only one valid Expo project identity.

Remove any stale or conflicting:
- projectId
- updates.url
- owner
- slug
- package name
- Rork Expo project reference
- old EAS project reference
- duplicated expo-updates configuration

==================================================
3. STANDARDIZE app.json / app.config
==================================================

Use one authoritative configuration equivalent to:

{
  "expo": {
    "name": "IVX Holdings",
    "slug": "ivx-holdings",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "runtimeVersion": {
      "policy": "appVersion"
    },
    "updates": {
      "enabled": true,
      "checkAutomatically": "ON_LOAD",
      "fallbackToCacheTimeout": 0
    },
    "android": {
      "package": "<CURRENT_REAL_ANDROID_PACKAGE>",
      "versionCode": <INCREMENT_VERSION_CODE>
    },
    "extra": {
      "eas": {
        "projectId": "<CURRENT_REAL_EAS_PROJECT_ID>"
      }
    }
  }
}

Do not invent a new package or project ID.

If native dependencies changed, increment app version/runtimeVersion and create a new APK. Do not send an OTA update that requires native code to an older binary.

==================================================
4. STANDARDIZE eas.json
==================================================

Ensure the actual Android profiles contain explicit channels:

{
  "build": {
    "development": {
      "developmentClient": true,
      "distribution": "internal",
      "channel": "development"
    },
    "preview": {
      "distribution": "internal",
      "channel": "preview"
    },
    "production": {
      "channel": "production"
    }
  }
}

Verify production is mapped to the intended branch:

eas channel:view production

If incorrect:

eas channel:edit production --branch production

==================================================
5. REMOVE THE BROKEN UPDATE
==================================================

Identify the last known-good update group and the failing update group.

If the newest update is corrupt, incompatible, or has missing assets, roll back by republishing the last known-good group:

eas update:republish --group <LAST_GOOD_UPDATE_GROUP>

Do not republish a broken bundle.

Confirm that every Android asset URL returns HTTP 200 and is downloadable.

==================================================
6. BUILD A CLEAN APK WITH EMBEDDED FALLBACK
==================================================

Delete generated/cache files:

rm -rf node_modules
rm -rf .expo
rm -rf android/.gradle
rm -rf android/app/build
rm -rf android/build
rm -rf .eas
rm -rf dist

Reinstall and validate:

npm ci
npx expo-doctor
npx expo export --platform android --clear

Create a completely new Android preview APK:

eas build --platform android --profile preview --clear-cache

The new APK must contain a valid embedded JavaScript bundle so the app can start even when OTA update servers are unavailable.

==================================================
7. TEST WITHOUT OTA UPDATES FIRST
==================================================

Before publishing another OTA update:

1. Uninstall every old IVX APK from the device.
2. Install the newly generated APK.
3. Put the Android device in airplane mode.
4. Open the app.
5. Verify the embedded bundle loads successfully.
6. Close and reopen the app three times.
7. Restore internet.
8. Open again.
9. Verify no “Failed to download remote update” screen appears.

If the APK cannot launch offline, the embedded update/build is defective. Fix it before continuing.

==================================================
8. PUBLISH A MATCHING TEST UPDATE
==================================================

Only after the clean APK passes offline testing, publish:

eas update --channel preview --platform android --message "Android OTA download recovery"

Verify that the published update has the exact same runtimeVersion as the installed preview APK.

Then:

1. Open the APK online.
2. Allow the update to download.
3. Restart the application.
4. Confirm the update loads.
5. Restart three additional times.
6. Test Wi-Fi.
7. Test cellular data.
8. Test temporary network loss.
9. Confirm the app falls back instead of showing a fatal screen.

==================================================
9. ADD NON-FATAL UPDATE ERROR HANDLING
==================================================

The application must never replace the entire UI with a fatal update-download screen.

Add update diagnostics and safe fallback:

- catch update-check errors
- catch update-download errors
- record the exact error locally
- continue with the embedded or cached bundle
- never crash solely because OTA downloading failed
- display an optional non-blocking “Update unavailable” notice
- include update ID, channel, runtimeVersion and timestamp in diagnostics

==================================================
10. VERIFY NETWORK AND ANDROID SETTINGS
==================================================

Audit:

- android.permission.INTERNET exists
- no invalid network security configuration
- no blocked HTTP asset URLs
- no expired SSL certificate
- no redirect loop
- no localhost or private IP update URL
- no old localtunnel URL
- no 169.254.x.x URL
- no Metro development URL inside the release APK
- no inaccessible Rork-hosted update URL
- device date/time is correct
- Android WebView and Play Services are current

Every production/update URL must be HTTPS and publicly reachable.

==================================================
11. FINAL REQUIRED PROOF
==================================================

Return all of the following:

A. Root cause
- exact exception
- exact failed URL
- HTTP status
- whether failure was manifest, bundle, asset, SSL, DNS, channel or runtime mismatch

B. Configuration
- EAS project ID
- Android package
- build profile
- channel
- branch
- APK runtimeVersion
- update runtimeVersion

C. Build evidence
- GitHub repository
- branch
- commit SHA
- EAS build ID
- APK URL
- Android versionCode
- build completion timestamp

D. Update evidence
- EAS update ID
- update group ID
- channel
- runtimeVersion
- Android platform confirmation

E. Device evidence
- screenshot of successful app launch
- offline launch result
- Wi-Fi launch result
- cellular launch result
- three cold-restart results
- adb log showing no fatal expo-updates exception

STATUS RULE:

Do not report COMPLETE until the newly installed APK opens offline and online on a real Android device without:

java.io.IOException: Failed to download remote update
